Defining ‘rivers’ for the galamsey study — OSM vs MERIT Hydro vs remote sensing

An exploration on the Ankobra basin — notes and open questions

Till Meissner

02 Juli 2026

1 What this research project is about

(Context for a reader coming to this cold — feel free to skip to the specific question.)

Galamsey is small-scale, largely unlicensed artisanal gold mining, which has expanded rapidly across southern Ghana over the last two decades. Beyond clearing vegetation and reshaping valley floors, it discharges mercury, suspended sediment and turbidity into rivers, with widely reported consequences for water quality and for farmland downstream.

This research project maps galamsey and studies its drivers and consequences, in two phases:

  1. Remote sensing — classifying mine presence from Landsat/Sentinel-2 imagery via Google Earth Engine (1995–2025, all Ghana).
  2. Spatial & economic analysis — where mining occurs, why it clusters spatially, and what it does to the surrounding environment and economy.

The current analysis uses the Barenblitt et al. (2021) satellite-derived mining-extent maps for SW Ghana (annual, 2007–2017) as an interim data source, on a hexagonal grid (~5 km cells) over the study region.

The specific piece this note supports is an event study of the downstream effect of mining: does galamsey upstream of a location degrade vegetation — and, by proxy, agricultural productivity and water quality — downstream, through waterborne transport of mercury and sediment? We proxy the downstream outcome with satellite vegetation greenness (NDVI). To run that design we must know, for every location, which mines are hydrologically upstream of it. That is entirely a question of how we define the river network and its direction of flow — which is what this note is about, and where we would most value a geologist’s / hydrologist’s input.

2 The question we are unsure about

This is a working note, not a settled write-up. We are trying to decide what should count as a “river” for the galamsey study, and we would value a geologist’s / hydrologist’s view on whether we are framing the problem sensibly at all.

“River” enters the analysis in two different places, and it is not obvious the same definition should serve both:

  1. A proximity covariate — dist_river_km. Distance to the nearest watercourse as a predictor of where galamsey occurs (alluvial gold sits in and beside channels). Here we need channel geometry.
  2. A directed flow graph — the event-study treatment. The upstream→downstream design (a_05_event_study.Rmd) asks whether mining upstream of a hex degrades vegetation/water downstream. Here we need channels and flow direction, and the network must respect drainage divides.

Three candidate data sources encode “river” quite differently:

Source What a “river” is Flow direction? Small tributaries?
OSM waterways A line a human mapped and labelled No Patchy — depends on mapping effort
MERIT Hydro A DEM cell whose upstream drainage area exceeds a threshold Yes (D8) Yes, down to the chosen threshold
Remote sensing (MNDWI / JRC) A pixel observed to hold surface water No Only where water is visible

The worry that motivates this note. In the waterborne event study, does the pollution signal from large rivers get diluted if we also label as “treated” many hexes that sit only on tiny streams — which may not actually transport mercury/sediment far? But if we raise the river threshold to keep only large channels, a large share of mining hexes fall out of the sample. Is that the right way to think about the trade-off, and how should it be handled? The section The event-study trade-off is our attempt; we are genuinely unsure and would like your read.

We work on the Ankobra basin — the SW-Ghana river most associated with galamsey pollution, and the area for which we already have a MERIT clip. Leaflet maps are used throughout because the channel-vs-observed comparison is mostly visual (hence an .Rmd: leaflet embeds cleanly in a self-contained html_document).

3 Study area — the Ankobra corridor

# MERIT clip = the analysis window (the Ankobra corridor, native EPSG:4326).
hydro <- rast(merit_path)
names(hydro) <- c("dir", "upa", "wth", "elv")
dir_r <- hydro[["dir"]]; upa_r <- hydro[["upa"]]; wth_r <- hydro[["wth"]]; elv_r <- hydro[["elv"]]

merit_ext_ll  <- st_as_sf(as.polygons(ext(upa_r), crs = "EPSG:4326"))
merit_ext_utm <- st_transform(merit_ext_ll, UTM30N)

osm <- st_read(osm_path, quiet = TRUE) |> clean_names() |> st_make_valid() |>
  st_transform(4326)
osm_win <- suppressWarnings(st_crop(osm, st_bbox(merit_ext_ll)))

ankobra_river <- osm_win |>
  filter(str_detect(coalesce(name, ""), regex("ankobra", ignore_case = TRUE)))

gal <- st_read(barenblitt_path, quiet = TRUE) |> clean_names() |> st_make_valid() |>
  st_transform(4326)
mt_col <- intersect(c("mine_type", "minetype"), names(gal))[1]
gal_art <- gal[gal[[mt_col]] == 1, ]
gal_win <- suppressWarnings(st_crop(gal_art, st_bbox(merit_ext_ll)))

cat(sprintf("MERIT window: %.2f x %.2f deg | %d OSM segments | %d Ankobra-named | %d galamsey polys\n",
            diff(st_bbox(merit_ext_ll)[c(1,3)]), diff(st_bbox(merit_ext_ll)[c(2,4)]),
            nrow(osm_win), nrow(ankobra_river), nrow(gal_win)))
## MERIT window: 0.35 x 1.01 deg | 45 OSM segments | 12 Ankobra-named | 1540 galamsey polys

The MERIT clip is a narrow N–S strip: the Ankobra flows broadly south to the Gulf of Guinea. Galamsey polygons (Barenblitt 2019 artisanal) cluster along the valley — which is, of course, part of what we are trying to understand.

4 Approach 1 — OSM waterways

OpenStreetMap waterway=* lines are crowd-sourced, human-labelled features. They give real, named rivers and a waterway type we can filter on. d_03_waterways.R keeps only natural watercourses; artificial canals/drains/ditches presumably cannot host alluvial gold (is that a safe assumption? — one for discussion).

natural    <- c("river", "stream", "brook", "wadi", "tidal_channel", "stream_pool", "flowline")
artificial <- c("canal", "drain", "ditch", "lock_gate", "fish_pass", "weir", "dam",
                "sluice_gate", "pressurised", "penstock")
osm_win <- osm_win |>
  mutate(ww_class = case_when(waterway %in% natural ~ "natural watercourse",
                              waterway %in% artificial ~ "artificial channel",
                              TRUE ~ "other / review"))

osm_win |> st_drop_geometry() |> count(waterway, ww_class, sort = TRUE) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "OSM waterway types within the Ankobra window.")
OSM waterway types within the Ankobra window.
waterway ww_class n
river natural watercourse 20
stream natural watercourse 18
drain artificial channel 7 
class_pal <- colorFactor(c(col_osm_nat, col_osm_art, col_osm_oth),
                         domain = c("natural watercourse", "artificial channel", "other / review"))
leaflet(osm_win) |>
  addProviderTiles("Esri.WorldImagery", group = "Satellite") |>
  addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron", group = "Light") |>
  addPolylines(color = ~class_pal(ww_class), weight = 2, opacity = 1,
               label = ~paste0(coalesce(name, "(unnamed)"), " — ", waterway),
               group = "OSM waterways") |>
  addPolygons(data = gal_win, color = "white", weight = 1.2, fillColor = col_gal,
              fillOpacity = 0.65, group = "Galamsey (Barenblitt)") |>
  addLegend(pal = class_pal, values = ~ww_class, title = "OSM class", position = "bottomright") |>
  addLayersControl(baseGroups = c("Satellite", "Light"),
                   overlayGroups = c("OSM waterways", "Galamsey (Barenblitt)"),
                   options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE))

OSM gives us real named rivers and a type field, for free. But coverage seems to be a function of mapping effort rather than hydrology — small tributaries (where alluvial galamsey may begin) look unevenly mapped, there is no flow direction, and “river” here mixes trunk rivers with tiny mapped streams while omitting unmapped ones. It may be fine as a proximity measure; it cannot build the directed flow graph.

5 Approach 2 — MERIT Hydro

MERIT Hydro (Yamazaki et al. 2019, ~90 m) is a DEM-derived, physically-modelled river network, with four bands used differently in this project:

Band Meaning Use
dir D8 flow direction (which neighbour each cell drains to) The flow graph (upstream/downstream)
upa Upstream drainage area (km²) accumulated through each cell Defining channels via a threshold
wth Satellite/OSM-fused river width (m); 0 = no mapped channel A stricter, validated channel mask
elv Hydrologically-adjusted elevation HAND / vertical drop (not morphometry)

5.1 Why we use a upa threshold to define a “river” (and where we are unsure)

upa is the catchment area draining through each cell, from flow accumulation over the D8 pointer. Headwater cells have a tiny upa; moving downstream, tributaries merge and upa grows monotonically. Our reasoning is that a river is where concentrated flow persists, so a cell is “channel” when its upa exceeds a minimum catchment. On that logic the threshold is the smallest upslope area we are willing to call a channel:

  • Low threshold → a dense network reaching every gully (in the limit, every cell is a “stream”).
  • High threshold → only the major trunk rivers survive.

We have been treating the threshold as a modelling choice calibrated against OSM. But is upstream area even the right variable to threshold on? A geologist might prefer discharge, stream order, channel width, or a stream-power criterion — upa is a proxy for “how much water”, not a measurement of it. We would welcome a view on what the physically meaningful cut-off is for “this is a river that can move sediment and contaminants”.

upa_vals <- values(upa_r, na.rm = TRUE)
qtab <- tibble(quantile = c("50%", "90%", "99%", "99.9%", "max"),
               upa_km2  = round(quantile(upa_vals, c(.5, .9, .99, .999, 1)), 2))
knitr::kable(qtab, caption = "Distribution of upstream drainage area (upa, km²) across Ankobra cells.")
Distribution of upstream drainage area (upa, km²) across Ankobra cells.
quantile upa_km2
50% 0.01
90% 0.38
99% 68.88
99.9% 7392.11
max 8409.39 
tibble(upa = upa_vals[upa_vals > 0.05]) |>
  ggplot(aes(upa)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#2171B5") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(10, 50), colour = c("#E67E22", "#C0392B"), linetype = 2) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = comma) +
  annotate("text", x = 10, y = Inf, label = "ROUTE_KM2 = 10", vjust = 1.5, hjust = 1.05,
           colour = "#E67E22", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = 50, y = Inf, label = "STREAM_KM2 = 50", vjust = 3, hjust = -0.05,
           colour = "#C0392B", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Where do the two working thresholds sit?",
       x = "Upstream drainage area (km², log scale)", y = "cells") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

5.2 The channel network at several thresholds

THRESH <- c(5, 10, 20, 50, 500)
thr_summary <- map_dfr(THRESH, \(t) {
  n <- sum(upa_vals > t)
  tibble(`upa >` = paste0(t, " km²"), `channel cells` = n,
         `% of grid` = round(100 * n / length(upa_vals), 2))
})
knitr::kable(thr_summary,
             caption = "Channel-cell count by upa threshold. Lower cut = denser network reaching further up the tributaries.")
Channel-cell count by upa threshold. Lower cut = denser network reaching further up the tributaries.
upa > channel cells % of grid
5 km² 17008 3.27
10 km² 12173 2.34
20 km² 9247 1.78
50 km² 6180 1.19
500 km² 2686 0.52 
thr_show <- c(10, 50)                       # ROUTE_KM2 vs STREAM_KM2
thr_cols <- c(col_upa10, col_upa50)
m <- leaflet() |>
  addProviderTiles("Esri.WorldImagery", group = "Satellite") |>
  addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron", group = "Light")
for (i in seq_along(thr_show)) {
  mask_i <- ifel(upa_r > thr_show[i], 1L, NA)
  pal_i  <- colorNumeric(thr_cols[i], domain = c(0, 1), na.color = "transparent")
  m <- addRasterImage(m, mask_i, colors = pal_i, opacity = 0.95, project = TRUE,
                      group = sprintf("MERIT upa > %g km²", thr_show[i]))
}
m |>
  addPolylines(data = filter(osm_win, ww_class == "natural watercourse"),
               color = col_osm_nat, weight = 1.5, opacity = 0.9, group = "OSM natural") |>
  addPolygons(data = gal_win, color = "white", weight = 1.2, fillColor = col_gal,
              fillOpacity = 0.65, group = "Galamsey") |>
  addLegend(colors = c(col_upa10, col_upa50, col_osm_nat, col_gal),
            labels = c("MERIT upa > 10", "MERIT upa > 50", "OSM natural", "Galamsey"),
            title = "Layer", position = "bottomright") |>
  addLayersControl(baseGroups = c("Satellite", "Light"),
                   overlayGroups = c("MERIT upa > 10 km²", "MERIT upa > 50 km²",
                                     "OSM natural", "Galamsey"),
                   options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)) |>
  hideGroup("MERIT upa > 50 km²")

Toggling the layers: at upa > 50 km² we get the trunk Ankobra and its main tributaries, which looks close to the OSM natural “river” lines (this is roughly why STREAM_KM2 = 50 was chosen). Dropping to upa > 10 km² fills in a finer tributary network that OSM misses and that many galamsey polygons sit on. Which of these is the “real” river network for pollution transport is exactly what we are unsure about.

5.3 Why two thresholds, and the tension between them

The project currently uses two cuts for the two jobs from the introduction:

  • STREAM_KM2 = 50 — the “river” label. Roughly where 4th-order tributaries begin; reproduces the OSM natural-river network.
  • ROUTE_KM2 = 10 — the flow-graph routing cut. Lower, so the directed graph reaches smaller tributaries. The stated justification (d_04_merit.R §11g): at upa > 50 about 23% of mined hectares fall off the channel network (never routed downstream), versus ~5.5% at upa > 10.

The d_04 §11j sweep over {2, 5, 10, 20} km² reports that the per-hex upstream-exposure ranking is fairly stable across cuts, which we took to mean the exact value is not critical for the routing. But that robustness is about connectivity, not about how much pollution a channel can carry — which is the concern in the next section.

6 The event-study trade-off: small streams vs. sample size

This is the heart of the note. In the waterborne design, a hex is (loosely) a candidate for “waterborne treatment” if it sits on / near a channel that connects it to upstream mining. The channel threshold sets who is in that sample, and it cuts two ways:

  • A low threshold (dense network). Almost every mining hex counts as “on a river”. But many of those channels are tiny — headwater streams with little discharge. If such streams do not actually transport mercury and sediment any meaningful distance, then we are labelling a lot of hexes as waterborne-connected when physically they are not. In regression terms this looks like measurement error in the treatment, which typically attenuates the estimated effect toward zero — the genuine signal from large, transport-capable rivers gets averaged together with many false-positive “treated” hexes.
  • A high threshold (sparse network). The remaining channels are genuinely big rivers, so a “treated” hex is more plausibly actually exposed. But now a large share of mining hexes are no longer on a river at all and drop out of the waterborne sample. That costs power, and — more subtly — it may change which mines are in the sample (mines on big rivers may differ systematically from mines on small streams: size, accessibility, industrial vs artisanal), so it is not just a power loss but a possible selection/composition change.

Is this the right framing? We think the trade-off is real, but we are not sure “pick one threshold” is the right response, for two reasons:

  1. The threshold conflates two things — connectivity (is this hex hydrologically linked to a mine at all?) and dose (how much contaminant could actually arrive?). A binary on/off cut forces one number to do both jobs.
  2. Transport capacity is continuous, not a step. Whether a stream moves galamsey pollution presumably rises smoothly with discharge / stream power, rather than switching on at 10 or 50 km².

Let us first see the trade-off in the Ankobra data, then list options — as questions, not answers.

6.1 How many mining hexes are “at a river” at each threshold?

We take the 5 km hexes intersecting the Ankobra window, flag the mining hexes (Barenblitt artisanal, art_ha > 0), and ask, for a grid of thresholds, whether each mining hex contains a MERIT channel cell with upa above that threshold (i.e. does the mine sit on a channel that “counts” as a river at that cut). This is the source side of the waterborne channel: a mine only injects pollution into the routed network if it is on the network.

cs <- readRDS(hex_cache_path)
hex_sf <- cs$hex_sf
ha     <- cs$hex_analysis

hex_win <- hex_sf[lengths(st_intersects(hex_sf, merit_ext_utm)) > 0, ] |>
  left_join(select(ha, hex_id, art_ha, any_art), by = "hex_id")

# Max upstream area among each hex's MERIT cells → does it contain a channel > t?
hex_win$max_upa <- terra::extract(upa_r, vect(st_transform(hex_win, 4326)),
                                  fun = max, na.rm = TRUE)[, 2]
mine <- filter(hex_win, art_ha > 0)

THRG <- c(0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100)
elig_tab <- map_dfr(THRG, \(t) tibble(
  `upa threshold (km²)`      = t,
  `mining hexes`             = nrow(mine),
  `at a river (eligible)`    = sum(mine$max_upa > t, na.rm = TRUE),
  `% retained`               = round(100 * mean(mine$max_upa > t, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
  `% dropped`                = round(100 * mean(!(mine$max_upa > t), na.rm = TRUE), 1)
))
knitr::kable(elig_tab, caption = paste0(
  "Ankobra window: of ", nrow(mine), " mining (artisanal) 5 km hexes, how many still sit on a ",
  "channel as the river threshold rises. Raising the cut from 10 to 50 km² drops ~1 in 4 mining hexes ",
  "out of the waterborne sample."))
Ankobra window: of 102 mining (artisanal) 5 km hexes, how many still sit on a channel as the river threshold rises. Raising the cut from 10 to 50 km² drops ~1 in 4 mining hexes out of the waterborne sample.
upa threshold (km²) mining hexes at a river (eligible) % retained % dropped
0 102 102 100.0 0.0
5 102 96 94.1 5.9
10 102 86 84.3 15.7
20 102 74 72.5 27.5
50 102 62 60.8 39.2
100 102 51 50.0 50.0 
elig_tab |>
  ggplot(aes(`upa threshold (km²)`, `% retained`)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2171B5", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2, colour = "#2171B5") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(10, 50), linetype = 2, colour = c("#E67E22", "#C0392B")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 100)) +
  labs(title = "The sample-size cost of a stricter river threshold",
       subtitle = "Share of Ankobra mining hexes that remain 'on a river' as the upa cut rises",
       x = "upa threshold (km²)", y = "% of mining hexes retained") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

# How big is the channel each mining hex sits on? This is the flip side of the worry:
# how many mining hexes are only on small streams?
pct_small10 <- round(100 * mean(mine$max_upa <= 10, na.rm = TRUE))
pct_small50 <- round(100 * mean(mine$max_upa <= 50, na.rm = TRUE))
mine |> st_drop_geometry() |>
  ggplot(aes(pmax(max_upa, 0.1))) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "#8856A7") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(10, 50), linetype = 2, colour = c("#E67E22", "#C0392B")) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "What size river does each mining hex sit on?",
       subtitle = sprintf("~%d%% of mining hexes sit on a channel ≤ 10 km²; ~%d%% ≤ 50 km² (upstream area).",
                          pct_small10, pct_small50),
       x = "Largest channel in the hex — upstream area (km², log scale)",
       y = "mining hexes") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

# Where are the eligible vs dropped mining hexes, as the threshold rises?
thr_facets <- c(5, 10, 20, 50)
osm_nat_ll <- filter(osm_win, ww_class == "natural watercourse")
elig_long <- map_dfr(thr_facets, \(t) hex_win |>
  st_transform(4326) |>
  mutate(threshold = factor(paste0("upa > ", t, " km²"),
                            levels = paste0("upa > ", thr_facets, " km²")),
         status = case_when(art_ha > 0 & max_upa > t ~ "mine — on a river (kept)",
                            art_ha > 0               ~ "mine — off network (dropped)",
                            TRUE                     ~ "no mine")))

ggplot(elig_long) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = status), colour = "grey85", linewidth = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = osm_nat_ll, colour = "#3B8ED0", linewidth = 0.3, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ threshold, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("mine — on a river (kept)"    = "#1A9850",
                               "mine — off network (dropped)" = "#D73027",
                               "no mine"                      = "grey92"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Which mining hexes stay in the waterborne sample as the river threshold rises",
       subtitle = "Ankobra window, 5 km hexes. Green = mine on a channel at that cut; red = mine dropped; blue = OSM natural rivers.",
       caption = "Barenblitt artisanal mining; MERIT Hydro upa. Higher threshold = more red (mines fall out).") +
  theme_void(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 7))

The red hexes are the cost of a stricter definition; the green are the “confidently on a real river” sample. The histogram is the other side of the coin: it shows how many mines sit only on small channels in the first place.

6.2 Ways this might be addressed — options and questions

We are unsure which of these is right (or whether the premise holds). Candidates, roughly from least to most structural:

  1. Treat the threshold as a robustness axis, not a single choice. Estimate the downstream effect across the whole grid of cuts and report the path. If the effect strengthens as we restrict to larger rivers, that is arguably evidence for the waterborne mechanism (transport capacity matters), not a nuisance. If it is flat, the small-stream dilution worry may be overstated. Question: would you expect the effect to grow with river size if the mechanism is real?
  2. Use a continuous, flow-weighted dose instead of a binary “on a river”. Weight each mine’s contribution by the discharge / upa / channel width wth at the connecting reach, so a mine on the trunk Ankobra counts for more than one on a trickle. This keeps every mine in the sample (no drop-outs) while down-weighting small streams. (d_04 already prototypes a distance-decayed upstream exposure — this would extend it with a flow weight.) Question: is upa an acceptable proxy for transport capacity, or is discharge/stream power needed?
  3. Stratify by stream order / size class and estimate separately. Headwater vs mid-order vs trunk. This tests the mechanism directly rather than assuming a cut-off. MERIT can yield Strahler order (d_04 §10d). Question: is Strahler order the right stratifier, or something else?
  4. Interact treatment with a flow measure. Keep the low threshold (retain the sample) but interact upstream-mining treatment with the receiving reach’s upa/wth. If the effect loads on the high-flow interaction, the dilution worry is quantified rather than assumed.
  5. A physically-motivated decay/dilution kernel. Model contaminant concentration falling with downstream distance, with a decay length that depends on discharge — so small streams “transport” only a short way by construction. Question: does a simple dilution/settling model exist that we could parameterise for mercury vs. suspended sediment?
  6. Separate the two roles of the threshold. Use a low cut for connectivity (don’t drop mines from the graph) and a flow-based dose for intensity. This directly answers the two-jobs-one-number problem in the trade-off section.

6.2.1 Open questions for a geologist / hydrologist

These are the questions where we most need expertise, because they determine whether the trade-off above is even the binding one:

  • How far does galamsey pollution actually travel, and does that depend on stream size? Is mercury mostly sediment-bound (settles quickly → proximity/large-river matters) or dissolved / methylated (travels far → even small streams connect)? These imply very different “right” thresholds.
  • Is there a physical catchment/discharge size below which a mine’s load is simply not detectable downstream? If so, that number — not a statistical convenience — should set the threshold.
  • Do turbidity/sediment and mercury have different transport lengths? We use NDVI as the outcome, which may respond to sediment/turbidity more than to mercury — should the “river” definition differ by which pollutant/outcome we care about?
  • Is upstream drainage area an acceptable channel proxy at ~90 m, or should we be using channel width (wth), modelled discharge, or stream power instead?
  • Are dry-season vs. wet-season flows so different that an annual “is this a river” label is meaningless for parts of the network?

7 The other MERIT bands for the Ankobra

For completeness, the bands beyond the threshold discussion.

7.0.1 upa — upstream drainage area

plot(log1p(upa_r), col = hcl.colors(50, "Blues", rev = TRUE),
     main = "log(1 + upstream drainage area) [km²] — Ankobra")

upa rises smoothly downstream; the bright trunk is the Ankobra itself (max ≈ 8,400 km² at the outlet). Besides thresholding, its log is a natural “downstreamness” scalar (higher = further down the network).

7.0.2 wth — river width

wth_ch <- wth_r; wth_ch[wth_ch == 0] <- NA
plot(wth_ch, col = hcl.colors(30, "Blues", rev = TRUE),
     main = "MERIT river channel width (m) — Ankobra (0 = no mapped channel)")

wth_pos <- values(wth_r, na.rm = TRUE); wth_pos <- wth_pos[wth_pos > 0]
cat(sprintf("wth non-zero cells: %d (%.2f%% of grid) | median %.1f m | max %.1f m\n",
            length(wth_pos), 100 * length(wth_pos) / length(upa_vals),
            median(wth_pos), max(wth_pos)))
## wth non-zero cells: 2379 (0.46% of grid) | median 11.2 m | max 93.8 m

wth is a satellite + OSM-fused, validated river width, non-zero on only a small fraction of cells (the confidently-mapped channels). It might be a more physical channel definition than a upa threshold — it marks reaches observed to be rivers — but it will miss narrow headwater tributaries. It is tempting as a middle ground; whether it is better than a upa cut for our purpose is unclear to us.

pal_wth <- colorNumeric("plasma", domain = wth_pos, na.color = "transparent")
leaflet() |>
  addProviderTiles("Esri.WorldImagery", group = "Satellite") |>
  addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron", group = "Light") |>
  addRasterImage(ifel(upa_r > 10, 1L, NA),
                 colors = colorNumeric(col_upa10, c(0, 1), na.color = "transparent"),
                 opacity = 0.6, project = TRUE, group = "MERIT upa > 10 km²") |>
  addRasterImage(wth_ch, colors = pal_wth, opacity = 1, project = TRUE,
                 group = "MERIT width (wth)") |>
  addPolylines(data = filter(osm_win, ww_class == "natural watercourse"),
               color = col_osm_nat, weight = 1.5, opacity = 0.9, group = "OSM natural") |>
  addLegend(pal = pal_wth, values = wth_pos, title = "wth (m)", position = "bottomright") |>
  addLayersControl(baseGroups = c("Satellite", "Light"),
                   overlayGroups = c("MERIT width (wth)", "MERIT upa > 10 km²", "OSM natural"),
                   options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE))

7.0.3 elv — hydrologically-adjusted elevation

plot(elv_r, col = hcl.colors(50, "Terrain2"),
     main = "MERIT hydro-adjusted elevation (m) — Ankobra")

elv is nudged cell-by-cell to enforce downhill flow — right for HAND (vertical drop to nearest channel), but not for slope/morphometry (use MERIT-DEM there).

8 Approach 3 — remotely sensed surface water

A third notion of “river” is observed surface water — pixels that actually hold water. Two flavours: (1) our own MNDWI from Landsat/Sentinel-2 (rs01/rs02), which with NDTI can even flag turbid mining ponds; and (2) JRC Global Surface Water (JRC/GSW1_4/GlobalSurfaceWater), a validated occurrence layer (1984–2021, 30 m). Neither is downloaded yet, so the code below is a template (needs GEE auth) and is not evaluated on knit.

library(rgee)
bb  <- st_bbox(merit_ext_ll)
aoi <- ee$Geometry$Rectangle(c(bb[["xmin"]], bb[["ymin"]], bb[["xmax"]], bb[["ymax"]]))
occurrence <- ee$Image("JRC/GSW1_4/GlobalSurfaceWater")$select("occurrence")$clip(aoi)
mndwi <- ee$ImageCollection("COPERNICUS/S2_SR_HARMONIZED")$
  filterBounds(aoi)$filterDate("2023-11-01", "2024-03-01")$
  filter(ee$Filter$lt("CLOUDY_PIXEL_PERCENTAGE", 40))$median()$
  normalizedDifference(c("B3", "B11"))$rename("mndwi")$clip(aoi)
# Export to Drive, read back with terra::rast(), overlay in leaflet as above.

This observes actual water, including mining ponds the other sources never encode — but it detects water bodies, not a connected channel graph; it is seasonal and cloud-limited; narrow rivers under canopy are invisible; and it gives no flow direction. It seems best as a complement (validating MERIT channels, flagging ponds) rather than the definition of the network.

9 Does the definition move dist_river_km?

A practical check: on the 5 km hexes inside the Ankobra window, how much does distance-to-nearest-river shift across definitions? (Mirrors d_03_waterways.R §5–7, restricted to the MERIT extent.)

cent    <- st_centroid(hex_sf)
inside  <- lengths(st_intersects(cent, merit_ext_utm)) > 0
hex_in  <- cent[inside, ]

osm_all_utm <- st_transform(osm_win, UTM30N)
osm_nat_utm <- st_transform(filter(osm_win, ww_class == "natural watercourse"), UTM30N)
chan_pts <- function(thr) {
  m <- ifel(upa_r > thr, 1L, NA)
  suppressWarnings(as.points(m) |> st_as_sf() |> st_transform(UTM30N))
}
merit10 <- chan_pts(10); merit50 <- chan_pts(50)
wth_pts <- suppressWarnings(as.points(ifel(wth_r > 0, 1L, NA)) |> st_as_sf() |>
                              st_transform(UTM30N))

dist_to <- function(pts, tgt) {
  idx <- st_nearest_feature(pts, tgt)
  as.numeric(st_distance(pts, tgt[idx, ], by_element = TRUE)) / 1000
}
dcmp <- tibble(
  hex_id          = hex_sf$hex_id[inside],
  `OSM all`       = dist_to(hex_in, osm_all_utm),
  `OSM natural`   = dist_to(hex_in, osm_nat_utm),
  `MERIT upa>50`  = dist_to(hex_in, merit50),
  `MERIT upa>10`  = dist_to(hex_in, merit10),
  `MERIT wth>0`   = dist_to(hex_in, wth_pts)
)
dcmp |>
  pivot_longer(-hex_id, names_to = "definition", values_to = "dist_km") |>
  group_by(definition) |>
  summarise(mean = round(mean(dist_km), 2), median = round(median(dist_km), 2),
            max = round(max(dist_km), 2), .groups = "drop") |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Per-hex distance to nearest river (km) by definition — Ankobra window.")
Per-hex distance to nearest river (km) by definition — Ankobra window.
definition mean median max
MERIT upa>10 1.38 1.19 4.78
MERIT upa>50 3.03 2.66 9.61
MERIT wth>0 7.36 6.59 22.73
OSM all 4.71 3.77 20.59
OSM natural 4.73 3.78 20.59 
dcmp |>
  pivot_longer(c(`MERIT upa>50`, `MERIT upa>10`, `MERIT wth>0`),
               names_to = "merit_def", values_to = "merit_km") |>
  ggplot(aes(`OSM natural`, merit_km)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey60", linetype = 2) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.7, colour = "#2171B5") +
  facet_wrap(~ merit_def) + coord_equal() +
  labs(title = "Distance to river: OSM natural vs MERIT definitions (5 km hexes)",
       subtitle = "Below the 1:1 line = MERIT finds a nearer channel than OSM",
       x = "OSM natural (km)", y = "MERIT (km)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

The denser MERIT network (upa > 10) places almost every hex close to a channel; wth / upa > 50 behave more like the OSM natural rivers. Which is “better” again depends on the use, and on the mechanism questions in Open questions for a geologist.

10 Where we have landed — provisionally

Our current, tentative leaning (open to revision):

Use Leaning Why — and our uncertainty
Flow graph / upstream treatment MERIT dir + a low upa cut (10 km²) Only source with flow direction; the low cut keeps small-tributary mines in the graph. But whether those small channels should carry treatment weight is exactly the trade-off.
dist_river_km covariate MERIT upa > 50 or OSM natural (they agree) Reproduces the observed river network; robustness = swap between them.
Pollution-source / pond validation Remote sensing (MNDWI, JRC) Sees real standing water incl. galamsey ponds — a complement, not the network.

The part we are least sure about is the treatment-definition trade-off (above): whether to pick a threshold, sweep it, weight by flow, or stratify by stream size. Our instinct is that a continuous flow-weighted dose (option 2/4) is more honest than any single cut — but that rests on upa/wth being an acceptable proxy for how far galamsey pollution actually travels, which is a question for someone who knows the geochemistry. Feedback on the open questions would change what we build next.

Data: MERIT Hydro (Yamazaki et al. 2019); OpenStreetMap contributors; Barenblitt et al. (2021). Companion scripts: d_03_waterways.R (OSM), d_04_merit.R (MERIT flow graph), d_05_ndvi.R (Ankobra basin definition). See also 4_presentation/methodology_explainer.md (event-study designs).
